## History of European Ideas

Volume 30, Issue 2 , June 2004, Pages 149-166

# David Hume's legal theory: the significance of general laws

Author links open overlay panel Neil McArthur

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.histeuroideas.2004.03.004> [Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0191659904000312&orderBeanReset=true)

## Abstract

Hume is normally—and in my view, correctly—taken to be a legal conventionalist. However, the nature of Hume's conventionalism has not been well understood. Scholars have often interpreted David Hume as being largely indifferent to the specifics of the laws, so long as they accomplish their basic task of protecting people's property. I argue that this is not correct. Hume thinks certain systems of law will accomplish their purpose, of coordinating people's behaviour for the benefit of all, better than others. He introduces two concepts, which I call generality and convenience, to designate those features of the law that allow it to best accomplish its purpose. Of the two, generality is the more important. The ability to implement a system of what Hume calls “general laws” is a feature common to those governments he considers “civilized” rather than “barbarous.” A set of more specific criteria may be extracted from Hume's texts, which laws must meet if they are to be considered general. Many of the criteria Hume identifies later become associated with theorists of the so-called “rule of law.” Hume's conventionalism can thus be read an important development beyond that of Hobbes, one that lays a foundation upon which later theorists such as A.V. Dicey are able to build.

## Introduction

David Hume is normally—and in my view, correctly—taken to be a legal conventionalist. He believes that society's laws reflect an on-going agreement among its members to adhere to a set of rules that have arisen gradually, as people realize that their existence benefits everyone. However, the nature of Hume's legal conventionalism has not been well understood. Scholars have often interpreted him as being largely indifferent to the specifics of the laws, so long as they accomplish their basic task of protecting people's property. I propose to argue that this is not correct. Instead, I will argue that Hume does not hesitate to recommend certain types of legal systems over others, as better fulfilling the purpose for which law was established—that of protecting people's property.

Hume says that wise rulers will seek, as he puts it, to “balance” their states on “general laws.” <sup>1</sup> As I shall explain, the ability to implement a system of general laws is a feature common to those governments he considers “civilized” rather than “barbarous.” While scholars have ignored Hume's use of the expression “general laws,” it holds the key to understanding the depth of his legal theory, and the nature of his conventionalism. A re-evaluation of his views on the law shows his political philosophy to be both more original and more coherent than has generally been acknowledged.

My argument has several parts. After a brief discussion of Hume's views on the origins and function of the law, I explore in more detail Hume's use of the expression “general laws.” I then argue that for Hume, the task of “balancing” of a state on general laws consists in ensuring that the laws are executed according to a specific set of criteria—most notably that they are applied consistently to all relevant cases, and that everyone in the state is subject to them, including public officials. Following this, I explore the standard by which Hume thinks different systems of equivalently general laws may be judged relative to one another—a standard he calls “convenience.” I conclude with an exploration of Hume's views on legal reform.

## Section snippets

## Hume's conventionalism

According to the account Hume provides in the _Treatise of Human Nature_ (and re-iterates in the _Enquiry concerning the Principles of Morals_ ), society's laws are based on the conventions that evolve in order to solve what is now known as a coordination problem concerning property. People recognize it is in their interest to follow rules protecting their property, just so long as everyone else follows these rules as well. Thus, through time, a set of accepted practices emerge governing the

## General laws: the puzzle

In the context of physics and the study of nature, Hume uses the term “general laws” somewhat disparagingly, to refer to the objects of our misguided search for causal principles we can know with certainty. <sup>5</sup>

## General laws: the criteria

Assuming such a ruler of “great penetration and experience” does arise and sets out to improve her country's legal system, can she find any guidance from Hume's writing? If we study Hume's reflexions on the problem of law and government in his essays, his accounts of these exceptional monarchs, and (most fruitful of all) his analysis of the short-comings of England's laws through its history, we can extract from these texts a set of criteria that characterize systems of general laws.

I propose

## Convenience and legal reform

We must now address the second problem Hume identifies in balancing the state on general laws, which I above labeled [ii]: of discerning “what general laws are, upon the whole, attended with fewest inconveniencies.” Though I have said Hume suggests certain criteria an optimal system of laws will meet, he does not believe, as some natural law theorists have asserted, that civil laws must meet some prior standard in order to be legitimately called “laws.” Whatever acts result from the established

## Conclusion

There is a tension in Hume's writing between the ideal of a fully general and convenient set of laws, and the reality of society's slow evolution through trial and error. He makes several comments that suggest a legislator should have the ideal of a complete and permanent system of such laws before her.
